sentences.text: Well, we-- we went back to Marseilles after-- after the Spanish wouldn't let us in.
We went back to Marseilles, and then we took a ship.
To-- to Lisbon.
We wanted to get to Lisbon.
That was our objective.
Because that's where the ocean liner was waiting.
No.
No way.
Well, they just-- they-- they explained it and all.
Says, look, you just can't come in.
This is-- Franco-- Franco has-- has given us an order.
We can't let any-- any-- any Jews come in.
Yes.
Well, who else would-- who-- who else would-- would cross the border?
I mean, except for the Spanish, the Spanish Republicans.
But we didn't look like Spanish Republicans.
Right.
We went back to Marseilles.
We got-- we got ourself a merchant ship.
I guess my father had to pay for it.
We weren't worried about it anymore.
In other words, we--we took a chance.
And-- and we-- we took risks, in other words.
And-- so-- we were on, you know.
We were--
No.
That-- believe me, no.
It didn't-- it didn't-- I mean, I did speak some German in that-- in that Quaker home.
But I tried French.
I tried as much-- my French is very limited.
But I-- I tried to speak French as much as possible.
My mother had a lot to do with it.
My mother-- see, that's where it helped.
My mother being over in the United States was able to get money.
She worked as a domestic.
But she also got help from HIAS to get-- to-- to get us out of here.
Yes.
Right.
Right.
No, I don't remember.
It-- in the fall of 1941.
Yeah.
I try to stay--
--away from people, you see.
I was trying to stay away from people.
I don't want to get involved in-- in-- having them recognize who I am-- recognize me, who I am.
In other words, it-- it was my job to stay away from this-- from the crowd.
Well, just Petain.
He was there in--
Marshal Petain.
He was the-- he-- he was the president of-- of France at that time.
Well, he was-- he was-- he was parading.
There was a parade or something, and-- and he was in a car.
And-- and I guess he was visiting Marseilles.
At that time?
Not too bad, believe it or not.
[LAUGHS] I mean, he's-- but what happened historically, of course, is different.
But--
Oh, they-- they still loved him as a hero.
He was the hero of World War I, remember.
Well, I guess-- let's say, probably another three months.
In other words, let me see, September, October, November.
About three months.
Well, we got ourselves some kind of apartment for the time being.
An apartment.
I don't know who paid for it, but it was an apartment.
And wasn't much of an apartment.
But we-- we able to-- to survive for three months.
Always fearful.
Always fearful.
The only time I really felt secure was when I came to the United States.
Well, follow our plans.
In other words, we were scheduled to be on that ocean liner.
And-- and since we couldn't make it to go to Lisbon, we decided to-- to go another route.
And we ended up in Casablanca.
That's where we had to wait about three weeks before-- before that ship came in.
By-- well, we took a-- a merchant ship.
And we stop-- made several stops.
And the British submarines protected us, believe it or not.
The British submarines protected us.
There were some other refugees.
Some other German Jews, French Jews.
You know, it's just--
I just--
No, they had to be mostly Jewish-- Jewish people.
People who had the money to pay.
And the only thing I-- I-- we did exciting was playing chess.
My father and myself, we-- we played a game of chess.
We-- I learned how to play chess.
Not too long.
I don't remember now.
But it was--
Look, it can't-- I mean, if it was-- it had to be about three weeks maybe.
Yeah.
Oh,yes.
We make stopover.
Oran, Algiers, Tangiers.
We stop-- made three stops before we ended up in Casablanca.
Well, the-- the-- was it the Free French that had-- that had control of Casablanca?
Well, once you got to Casablanca, you were fairly safe.
I mean, the-- well of course, Vichy, France-- the-- the Vichy French government was still in control of-- of-- of Casablanca.
I-- I really don't know.
I really don't know.
No.
The only thing we were afraid of is that the German submarines would be in-- in-- would be stopping us in the Mediterranean.
But the British took care of that.
Remember, that was during the day when Rommel was moving-- was-- was having-- was doing very well in-- in Africa-- North Africa.
So-- and it was during that time, period of time.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we just-- we were just lucky, that's all.
Well, nothing much.
We stayed there for about three weeks.
We-- we visited the-- the Jewish, the new Jewish Quarters and the old Jewish Quarters.
And the-- the new Jewish Quarter was not bad at all.
And-- but we stayed outside of Casablanca.
We had-- we had camped outside.
So--
Well, whoever was-- in other words, whoever went with us with the-- with the merchant ship was with us too.
And-- and sure enough, I don't remember now exactly what day it was, but the-- the ocean liner from Lisbon came and took us aboard.
And then with another trip went-- we went to-- our trip led us to Jamaica and Cuba.
The Serpa Pinto.
The Serpa Pinto.
I-- I guess it's a-- I'm not sure what the translation of it is.
But it's-- what is it, a horse?
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Well, anyway.
Serpa Pinto.
Well, my father--
Absolutely.
My father made all the arrangements.
Yeah.
What's that?
No.
All-- all I know is that I-- you know, I know my father made a mistake when he didn't get out in time.
But, since then, since he came back from Dachau, he was a very resourceful person.
And I--I tell you the truth, I think I--I owe my life to my father.
I really owe my life to him.
Yeah.
I mean, not home free, but I-- we felt--
Relief, right.
And after that, it was basically just a matter of--
Yeah.
It's not like the-- those modern cruise lines, you know.
The modern cruises, you know.
It's-- it was-- it was an ocean liner, but it was a small one.
And I guess [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they had play room-- play-- play room, you know.
They had a place to eat, a restaurant.
One restaurant and one game room.
But it was a small-- a small ocean liner.
Well, I don't remember exactly.
But I would say that there were about-- see, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] about 300.
Mostly Jews.
No.
No.
In other words, it-- it just didn't occur to me.
I mean, I-- again, I played my game of chess with my father to pass the time.
But we were-- what-- what we knew later on is that a few months later, all the Jews in southern France were deported to Eastern Europe.
And that's what we-- what we were able to avoid.
And if we would have stayed there a few more months, we would have been deported as well.
Jamaica, right.
Jamaica.
The British treated us well.
The British treated us very well.
And it was Cuba where-- in Cuba that they wouldn't let us go on board--
Three weeks.
Oh, yeah.
Yeah.
And-- but we didn't stay in a-- in a big-- in a city.
We stayed in a-- in a-- the British had some kind of a camp for us.
And they kept us there until-- until the ship came, until it was time to-- to go on board again.
Well, then our trip was to Cuba.
Well, the government would not let us in.
I mean, they wouldn't let us even board or go and-- and roam around or whatever.
You know, we had to stay on the ship.
So it was just a matter of a day or two before we moved on to New York.
Well, it-- my mother was there.
You know, in other words, it was a-- quite a traumatic experience again.
It was very--a very happy experience.
And we were on-- on the way of-- of becoming part of the American scene, you know?
Oh, yeah.
Yes.
Yes.
Right.
Well, we had a-- a lot of our relatives were there.
And for a few days, we stayed with them.
But my mother, remember, worked in-- in the Boston area.
So my-- and because my mother worked in the Boston area, my father and I also moved to that area, and we started a new life.
Well, the-- there were a lot of relatives.
A lot of cousins, by the way.
But we had really, to tell you the truth, nothing in common with them, because it's-- they-- they were still in their old way of living.
You know, in other words, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they-- I wanted to be away.
I mean, my father and I wanted to be away and start a new life.
And this was not going to-- if we would have stayed in New York, we would have-- we would have fallen into-- into their way of life.
And we wanted-- when we came to United States, we said we wanted it completely new.
In other words, I was looking for assimilation.
And-- and the-- my relatives over in New York were-- were back in the old days.
19-- yeah.
February, 1942.
Well, the last time--
--I saw her was in 1939.
Yeah, it was three years.
Yes.
About two and a half years.
Yes.
Yes.
We were-- it was quite a-- a reunion.
Went to--
Yeah, outside Boston.
Called Winthrop, Massachusetts.
And-- and that's where I stayed for-- for the-- for the-- the next 10 years.
No.
I-- I had-- I know I had to learn the English language.
And I made it my business to make sure I-- I-- I did learn.
And I had special-- well, special attention.
I had a special teacher who taught me how to speak, how to get rid of my accent, and whatever.
And I was determined to--
Seven years.
Seven years.
And it's just like-- the only education I got was basically real life experiences.
Yeah-- oh, and chess.
That's right.
Right.
Well, first of all, I-- I had a sort of a rough beginning, because they put me in a-- I think the fifth grade, you know, where I should have been much higher.
But I got some double promotions.
But still, I-- I graduated high school at age 20.
But it didn't make any difference, because when I went to college I had a lot of students that were my age, you know.
Well, I'm always considered--
--the German refugee over in-- in-- in Winthrop, Massachusetts, you know.
I mean, they accepted me.
The Jewish population accepted me.
But the-- and I became very active, very quickly.
But I had some problems with the-- like the McLaughlin gang, you know, that they--
Well, they were a whole bunch of 12 to 13 cousins, and I guess they-- they didn't want any stranger, or they-- they thought I was a Nazi or something, you know.
So they-- they gave me a hard time.
I once remember I had to run for my life from those kids, you know.
They were kind of-- they were pretty rough.
And they threw rocks at us and all that business-- at me.
But I had already made some friends.
So they were in the same-- they had the same problem that I had because they were supporting me.
Right-- at that point, it was anti-German.
I think anti-- I was a-- I was-- you know, they were not-- probably not smart enough to realize that I was-- I was a Jew.
Here, too.
Well,I knew it was--
It was a temporary situation.

I mean, once I-- I got away from-- from high school and into college, I met a lot of people that were concerned and-- and were sympathetic to my--to my-- way I feel, the way I thought and everything else.
Well, the only thing I can say is that I was-- the-- the-- the poorest subject that I had in school [LAUGHS] was English.
I-- I got bad grades in English.
Everything else was fine, but English.
In other words, I was not concerned-- I never thought for a minute that I was going to end up in-- in the United States.
That's-- that's what-- you know.
So you get-- you get an idea.
Well, high school-- I graduated high school 1948.
And-- and I got good grades, by the way.
I started getting good grades the-- the last four years-- 9th, 10th, 11th, 12th.
So I was pretty good.
And I-- I had choices of colleges.
And I thought the best thing-- best place to go was Tufts College--
--in Medford, Massachusetts.
I was delighted.
I was extremely happy.
I mean, thank goodness.
Now-- you know, now I can start concentrating on-- on my life.
Oh, absolutely.
You know, when-- when people made bonfires in-- in 1945?
I was very, very delighted.
I became aware that many of my-- my-- my family didn't make it.
In other words, as I said, about half of them did not make it.
Well, my-- my father had another brother, called Emil, who-- who did-- who ended up in a concentration camp, and I was very close to him.
And unfortunate.
Very unfortunate.
He-- he didn't make it.
He ended up in-- in Eastern Europe.
One of the camps in Eastern Europe.
Yeah.
Yeah.
That's right.
And I was very upset about it.
But you know, life is for the living.
In other words, you've got to start concentrating on-- on the living.
And I made it my business to-- to do something with my life.
Right.
Medford, Massachusetts.
Well, I had a teacher in high school that-- that was sort of sponsoring me.
And it was a good university.
So-- I was a good student, you know,so it was just a matter of-- he was sponsoring me.
And he said, why don't you go to Tufts, because I went to Tufts.
And I said, well, I'll take-- I mean, I knew very little about colleges at that time.
So I knew about Harvard and-- and Yale and all that.
But I was very pleased.
I was very happy there.
I was commuting.
I was commuting.
In other words, from my house.
In other words, I was not living on-- on the campus.
Now, when I went to Columbia University the next year-- I mean, after-- at '52 to '53--
No, No.
--graduated.
I graduated with a-- a B.A. in-- in History.
And then I went to Columbia University, who accepted me.
And then I got my Masters in Columbia University.
Yeah.
European History and American History.
Yeah.
Sure.
Oh.
Oh.
I'm not sure what exactly-- what made-- what my-- what exactly made me do this.
But the point is this, that history was my favorite subject, OK?
Maybe it-- all my travels and everything else had something to do with it.
But history became my-- my favorite subject, and that-- I stuck with it.
American history, European history.
And later on, you know, when I start teaching history, I even included Middle East and Medieval.
Yeah, a Masters.
Right.
Political Science.
The Masters was in Political Science.
I was interested in government.
And--
One year.
Just one year.
Yes, New York.
Columbia in one year.
I made it in one year, the Masters.
And then I went to the service for the Korean War.
That was between 1953 and '55.
And then I met my wife during that period.
And--
--we got married.
Well, I was mostly in-- in the-- in the shipping area.
In other words, I was shipping soldiers in-- out and in, you know.
Receiving them.
Soldiers that were-- came from all parts of-- all parts of the world.
And-- and I also took care of the finances.
The finances with the-- you know, how much these soldiers were getting, and everything else.
I would-- I would process those things.
I think the-- the reason that I never went overseas, basically, is because I met my wife during that time.
And when we got married--
We-- when we came-- when I came back, I had less than a year to go.
I didn't pay the-- Uncle Sam to-- to-- pay me-- pay them to go over to Korea.
Besides, 1953, a truce was signed anyway.
But no one knew exactly how long that truce was going to last.
Lillian.
Lillian.
And I met her during a-- during a dance in-- in-- in the Fort, in-- in Fort Dix.
And it was love at first sight.
I mean, we just had a proper chemistry.
Asbury Park, New Jersey.
In other words, when I-- when I got out, my first job was in Asbury Park, New Jersey.
I was the teacher.
I was a teacher, and-- and that's where we lived together.
In other words--
High school.
High school.
And they-- they also needed a-- a German teacher, so it worked out nicely.
History and German.
See, I-- I had a-- a minor in-- in German.
That's, of course, basically, from-- from my experience.
But it-- it worked out very nicely.
Yes.
Well, she was a secretary of-- in a South Philadelphia school.
Secretary.
But when she-- when she moved to Asbury Park, she wasn't doing anything except taking care that-- of my daughter, who was-- you know, was just one-- let me see.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Was just about a year old, my daughter.
What's that?
Yeah.
The first born, right.
Linda.
Linda Susan.
October, 1955.
What's that?
Linda?
Yeah.
I have Steven.
Who is 35.
And-- now, 35.
And Scott, who's 26, who's getting married in February.
Grandchildren?
Yes.
I have three of them.
One is Erin, and-- and Laura, and Diana.
Laura and Diana are-- belong to my son, Steven.
And-- and Erin is my-- my daughter's daughter.
